text: The surgeon begins a linear approach to excise an ischiorectal lipoma in the gluteal region of a 37-year-old woman. The CT scan reveals a 15-centimeter mass in the ischiorectal fossa, with no signs of infiltration or intratumoral vascularization.

The patient is positioned in the jackknife position for a perineal minimally invasive approach to excise the ischiorectal lipoma. The main surgeon and assistant are positioned on either side of the patient.
The surgeon makes a semicircular incision in the anodermal skin, then places a long star retractor to enhance exposure of the surgical site.

The surgeon performs dissection of the subcutaneous tissue using electrocautery, localizing the external sphincter. The ischiorectal mass is carefully dissected with bipolar energy and ligature, while preserving the sphincter apparatus. The inner wall of the ischiorectal fossa is desiccated.

The surgeon performs a careful digital dissection to separate the tumor from the obturator fascia and obturator internus, minimizing bleeding. Once fully separated, the mass is eviscerated from the surgical site.
The excised tumor measures approximately 14.8 by 7.2 centimeters. The pathology examination confirms it as a giant lipoma with clear resection margins, indicating no infiltration.

The surgeon removes the long star retractor and sutures the ischiorectal fossa with single stitches using 2-0 Vicryl. The skin is then approximated with a continuous intradermal suture using 3-0 monofilament.

The patient advised were to maintenance the skin clean and dry. She was discharged at two days postoperative without one infection or surgical site abscess. In the outpatient revision, the patient was asymptomatic without signs of anal incontinence.
